title: Lesson 394
transcript: Okay, we've been talking about transport architectures and topologies and signals, for example, between devices across a network. Something that becomes really important to think about is what's called performance metrics. In other words, understanding the quality the integrity of the transmissions for example that are going across your network you know and this is obviously important for planning but also for you know future endeavors and all kinds of things related to how the network is providing the level of quality that the organization requires, obviously, even from a security perspective, obviously focused on things like integrity of signal transmissions and services that are going across the network itself. So some of these you'll be very familiar with, right? Performance metrics related to things like bandwidth. And I know we hear this all the time, bandwidth, what is it? What is it? And, you know, obviously in networking, it's defined as the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted. It's like the size of your pipe, for example, is what is defined as bandwidth. In fact, I'm going to try and draw something here to make it more visually clear for all of us, right? Bandwidth as opposed to, for example, throughput. Because these are related to each other, but they're very different. And the way to think about them is the bandwidth is what the network can deliver. Throughput is what the network is actually delivering. And often what's used in order to relate to all of this is the pipe. example. And let me see if I can draw this. Probably not very good. Rob's already made fun of me that I can't draw, but he's absolutely right. Okay, here we go. All right. And this is like a water pipe. All right. So we've got a water pipe and that's the inside of it, basically. All right. And so the capacity of this How much water can flow through that pipe is what would be considered as, for example, bandwidth, right? So the maximum amount of water, for example, that can flow through this pipe based on its width and so on, is referred to as bandwidth the capacity so let's say we could or this had been built to deliver like for example 100 gallons of water per minute or something like that all right that would be its capacity which is what bandwidth really is all right so we can go like this and call this So that's the bandwidth, its capacity, what it can handle. Throughput is defined as the amount of water that actually flows through the pipe. So in here, we can put throughput. Oops. because how much water actually flows through that pipe even though it can handle you know 100 gallons per minute you know whatever we are piping through at a given time is the throughput itself all right so the throughput is Let me just read what it says here, right? So imagine you have a pipe with a maximum capacity of 100 gallons of water per minute. That's the bandwidth, that's the capacity, for example. If you start pumping water through the pipe at a rate of 50 gallons per minute, that would be the throughput right the actual throughput is 50 gallons per minute right even though the pipe can handle 100 gallons per minute we're only piping through it throughput 50 gallons per minute all right so that's the difference between obviously bandwidth and throughput and of course you know we can relate this to network terms in fact probably all of you are familiar with this based on the speeds that you have of your wireless networks at home and so on right So if you have a network connection with a bandwidth of 100 megabits per second, that means the connection has the capacity to transmit up to 100 megabits per second. All right, so that's obviously the bandwidth. However, the actual amount of data that is transmitted over the connection may be lower than 100 megabits per second. That would be referred to as the actual throughput. Right. So these are important measurements to be able to on a regular basis understand as far as the network is concerned. All right. Because in order to understand performance of a network and capacity planning and everything else for the future, that becomes pretty important. Planning how much data can be transmitted over the network at a given point in time. is obviously very important depending on the services that you want to provide through that network understanding bandwidth and throughput because throughput can be affected by any number of things. The actual performance, These two measurements are important to understand and differ. Let's keep going here because we've got some other things that I think are pretty important to understand. The next one is what's called signal to noise ratio, right? Signal to noise ratio. And this is a measurement of the signals, especially in things like Wi-Fi connections. In fact, we use signal to noise ratio that is normally measured in decibels. for anything that uses radio frequencies and radio signal transmissions like Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, RFID and 4G and 5G cellular capabilities, for example, a good measure of the quality of the signal, for example, that's being sent and received is what this is used to indicate. And what it indicates, what it measures, is it compares the level of desired signal to the level of the background noise, right? So the desired level to really the noise, which is the undesired level. And normally the way that this is measured is in decibels, dBs. So you have the desired level over what's called the noise floor. So you want that difference to be high because then that means the signal quality is also very high. So let me give you an example here. And so just before I give you an example, what we need to remember here is that in wireless, for example, your device's ability to distinguish signals as being legitimate from any background noise in the spectrum, for example, is referred to as the signal to noise ratio. And you want that to be high. So let me give you an example here. Let's say that we have a signal that is minus 65 decibels, right? But the noise floor is minus 80 decibels. All right. Your signal to noise ratio is basically the difference here, which in this case would be 15 decibels. Right. So that obviously gives us the difference between these. And as far as the industry is concerned. Anything that's, for example, anywhere from 25 decibels to 40 decibels is considered to be good, and anything above 41 decibels is considered to be excellent, right? Because then again, the device has the ability to distinguish between the noise and the actual decibels itself. All right. Okay. Let's finish up here. And talk about two more things that are specifically mentioned in the exam outline itself. And you might recognize these latency. Again, these are all measurements, performance measurements that apply to networks and the signals, for example, between devices. Latency can simply be defined. In fact, this is the perfect definition that comes from the common body of knowledge and therefore you might see it as exam questions. It's latency is considered or defined as being the time that it takes to receive frames. How long does it take, for example, for us to receive frames is considered to be latency, all right? And jitter, which you may have heard of, is the variation in those arrival times of frames, all right? So if we define latency as being the arrival time of frames, how long does it take for us to get those frames and arrive to our location, for example, the variation in those arrival times over a period of time is considered to be jitter. Right. So you don't want a lot of jitter, obviously, because it affects the arrival time of frames. Okay.
